---
title: "Foundations: discretization, devices, and contracts"
---

::: {.callout-tldr title="Fast path"}
This is rung 0 of the geometry unit — the page that turns "I know linear algebra"
into "I can read the rest of this book as one story." Three ideas hold up
everything that follows:

1. **A discrete object approximates a smooth one.** A triangle mesh stands in for
   a smooth surface; a voxel grid stands in for a region of space and the fields on
   it. A good *discrete operator* (a graph Laplacian, an angle defect, a finite
   difference) is a stand-in for a *continuous* one ($\Delta$, curvature,
   $\nabla$), and the quality bar is **convergence under refinement**: as the mesh
   subdivides or the grid fines, the discrete answer approaches the smooth truth.
   We check that against **analytic oracles** (a sphere's $H=1/r$; Gauss–Bonnet
   $\sum K A = 4\pi$) and real data by **class and correlation, never bit-parity**.
2. **Every routine carries an execution class** — **DIFF-JAX**, **HOST-CTOR**, or
   **HOST-QA** — and one load-bearing rule keeps the differentiable pipeline
   correct: *a HOST op must never be called inside a jitted loop*.
3. **The whole tier is cuSolver-free and XLA-lowered.** Closed-form small inverses,
   matrix-free conjugate gradient, a CPU-fallback solver seam, and `lax.gather` /
   `scatter` + `fori_loop` instead of custom kernels — these are not incidental,
   they shape the entire design.

If you read only one page before the ladder, read this one.
:::

This unit is written for a reader who is comfortable with **linear algebra** —
matrices, least squares, eigendecomposition, rank, transposes — but who has had
**no formal training in differential geometry, numerical methods, or graphics**.
Everything here is built up from the linear algebra you already own. Our aim across
the book is to carry you from *novice* (you can call a routine and pick the right
one) to *practitioner* (you understand why each answer converges and what each
argument means) to *developer* (you could implement it yourself). This page lays
the three planks the whole climb rests on. It is the sibling of the
[`nitrix.stats` foundations page](../foundations.qmd); the two units share one
house style.

## A motivating question {#sec-motivation}

Hold one concrete question in mind for the entire page. You have a reconstructed
**cortical surface** — a triangle mesh of roughly 150,000 vertices wrapping one
hemisphere — and you want to know, at every vertex, *how curved is the cortex
here?* Curvature separates the **gyral crowns** (the outward-bulging ridges) from
the **sulcal fundi** (the inward-folding valleys); it is the raw signal behind
folding-pattern registration, cortical-thickness sampling, and a dozen
morphometric features.

"Curvature" is a quantity from smooth differential geometry — it is defined for a
surface you can differentiate twice. But your surface is not smooth: it is a
finite list of vertices and triangles, with corners and flat faces and nothing to
differentiate. So the question splits into two:

1. What is the *continuous* thing we mean (mean curvature $H$ of a smooth
   surface)?
2. How do we compute a *discrete* number on the mesh that approaches it — and how
   do we know it does?

That gap, between a smooth quantity we can define and a discrete quantity we can
actually compute, is the subject of this entire unit. This page sets up the
framing; the later pages fill in the operators
([the cotangent Laplacian](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent),
[curvature](curvature.qmd#sec-mean)) and prove they land.

## Discrete objects approximate smooth ones {#sec-discretization}

Here is the single idea the rest of the book elaborates. **A discrete geometric
object is an approximation of a smooth continuous one, and a discrete operator is
an approximation of a continuous operator.**

Two kinds of discrete object recur throughout `nitrix`:

- A **triangle mesh** $(V, F)$ — vertices $V \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times 3}$ (the rows
  are points $v_i$) and faces $F \in \mathbb{Z}^{m\times 3}$ (each row lists the
  three vertex indices of a triangle) — approximates a smooth **2-manifold**: a
  surface, like the cortical sheet or a sphere. The mesh is a piecewise-flat
  membrane stretched over the smooth surface; refine it and the membrane hugs the
  surface ever more tightly.
- A **voxel grid** approximates a region of $\mathbb{R}^3$ and the scalar or
  vector **fields** defined on it — an image intensity, a tissue probability, a
  distance to a boundary. The grid is a sampling of a continuous field; refine the
  sampling and you recover the field.

The discrete object is never the smooth object. The craft of discrete geometry is
computing on the discrete object so that the geometric quantities you care about —
a Laplacian, a curvature, a distance — **converge to the continuous truth** as the
discretisation refines.

### Build by climbing: how a discrete operator gets earned

Take the gradient of a field, the simplest continuous operator. In the continuum,
the derivative of $f$ at a point is the limit of a difference quotient,
$f'(x) = \lim_{h\to 0}\big(f(x+h) - f(x)\big)/h$. The most obvious discrete
stand-in drops the limit and keeps a finite $h$ equal to the grid spacing:

$$
(\partial f)_i \;\approx\; \frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{h}.
$$ {#eq-forward}

This is the **forward difference**. It is the first thing a reader would invent,
and it works — but it converges *slowly*. Where it "breaks" is in accuracy: its
error shrinks only like $h$ (first order), so to halve the error you must double
the grid resolution, which in 3-D is eight times the memory. The cure is one idea:
make the stencil **symmetric** about the point, so the two leading error terms
cancel,

$$
(\partial f)_i \;\approx\; \frac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2h}.
$$ {#eq-central}

This is the **central difference**: the same operator, one symmetry better, and
now the error shrinks like $h^2$ (second order). That climb — *the obvious thing is
X; X is too crude in way Y; the cure is X plus one idea* — is exactly how every
operator in this unit is built. `nitrix.geometry.spatial_gradient` implements
@eq-central as its default (`scheme='central'`), with `'sobel'` and `'scharr'`
variants that additionally smooth across the off-axes for noise robustness, and a
`spacing` argument that puts the $2h$ denominator in physical units. It is the grid
analogue of the cotangent Laplacian for meshes.

The same story plays out on a mesh. The Laplacian of a smooth function measures how
much its value at a point departs from the average of its surroundings. The
simplest discrete stand-in averages over the **1-ring** $\mathcal{N}(i)$ — the
immediate neighbours of vertex $i$ — with equal weights (the *graph* or *umbrella*
Laplacian, what `mesh_laplacian_smooth` uses). That works on a perfectly regular
mesh but **breaks** on an irregular one: long thin triangles get the same vote as
fat ones, so the operator depends on how you happened to triangulate, not on the
underlying surface. The cure, again one idea, is to weight each neighbour by the
geometry of the triangles around the shared edge — the **cotangent weights** of
[the next page](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent). With them, two *different*
triangulations of the same smooth surface give *nearly the same* operator, and both
converge to the continuous Laplace–Beltrami operator under refinement.

### The icosphere: the canonical analytic test object

To say "converges to the truth" you need a discrete object whose smooth truth you
know exactly. The unit's workhorse is the **icosphere**: start from a regular
icosahedron (12 vertices, 20 faces) and repeatedly **subdivide** — split every
triangle into four by inserting edge midpoints, then project the new vertices back
onto the unit sphere. `nitrix.sparse.mesh.icosphere(n_iterations)` does exactly
this; the vertex count is $10\cdot 4^n + 2$, so `n=0` is the bare icosahedron and
`n=7` reaches 163,842 vertices (FreeSurfer's "ico7"). The companion
`icosphere_hierarchy(max_level)` returns the whole sequence of levels together with
the parent-child tables that record, for each fine vertex, which coarse edge it
midpoints — bookkeeping the cross-level pooling operators consume.

The icosphere is the test object because the smooth surface it approximates is the
**unit sphere**, whose geometry is closed-form: surface area $4\pi r^2$, mean
curvature $H = 1/r$ everywhere, Gaussian curvature $K = 1/r^2$, and the global
identity $\sum_i K A_i = 4\pi$ (a special case of Gauss–Bonnet for a genus-0
surface). These oracles come in two flavours, and the distinction matters. Most are
**metric** quantities — area, mean curvature — that the discrete object only
*approaches*: it gets them a little wrong on a coarse mesh and ever more right as
you refine, so the right thing to watch is a convergence march. But $\sum_i K A_i =
4\pi$ is special: it is **topological**, an integer invariant ($2\pi\chi$ with Euler
characteristic $\chi=2$ for a sphere) that the discrete angle-defect sum reproduces
*exactly*, to machine precision, at **every** refinement level — there is no march
to watch, which is itself the lesson (the discrete Gauss–Bonnet theorem is a
combinatorial identity, not an approximation). So @fig-foundations-convergence plots
the two quantities that genuinely converge — the surface area toward $4\pi r^2$ and
the cotangent mean-curvature estimate toward $H=1/r$ — and leaves the exact
topological identity to the prose.

```{python}
#| label: fig-foundations-convergence
#| fig-cap: "Left: a level-2 icosphere — a piecewise-flat mesh standing in for the smooth unit sphere. Right: two discrete quantities (total surface area, and the cotangent mean-curvature estimate) converge to their analytic values $4\\pi r^2$ and $H=1/r$ as the mesh is subdivided. Both ride a clean second-order (slope-2) trend on the log–log plot: halving the edge length quarters the error. (The level-0 icosahedron is vertex-transitive, so its mean-curvature mean is already exact by symmetry; the genuine march begins one level in.)"
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d.art3d import Poly3DCollection

# --- build an icosphere by hand (pure numpy: the same midpoint-subdivide rule
#     as nitrix.sparse.mesh.icosphere, reproduced here so the figure is
#     self-contained) -------------------------------------------------------
def icosahedron():
    phi = (1.0 + np.sqrt(5.0)) / 2.0
    v = np.array([
        [-1, phi, 0], [1, phi, 0], [-1, -phi, 0], [1, -phi, 0],
        [0, -1, phi], [0, 1, phi], [0, -1, -phi], [0, 1, -phi],
        [phi, 0, -1], [phi, 0, 1], [-phi, 0, -1], [-phi, 0, 1],
    ], dtype=np.float64)
    v /= np.linalg.norm(v, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    f = np.array([
        [0,11,5],[0,5,1],[0,1,7],[0,7,10],[0,10,11],
        [1,5,9],[5,11,4],[11,10,2],[10,7,6],[7,1,8],
        [3,9,4],[3,4,2],[3,2,6],[3,6,8],[3,8,9],
        [4,9,5],[2,4,11],[6,2,10],[8,6,7],[9,8,1],
    ], dtype=np.int64)
    return v, f

def subdivide(v, f):
    cache, verts = {}, v.tolist()
    def mid(i, j):
        key = (min(i, j), max(i, j))
        if key in cache:
            return cache[key]
        m = (v[i] + v[j]) / 2.0
        m /= np.linalg.norm(m)              # project back onto the sphere
        cache[key] = len(verts); verts.append(m.tolist())
        return cache[key]
    nf = []
    for a, b, c in f:
        ab, bc, ca = mid(a, b), mid(b, c), mid(c, a)
        nf += [[a, ab, ca], [b, bc, ab], [c, ca, bc], [ab, bc, ca]]
    return np.asarray(verts), np.asarray(nf, dtype=np.int64)

def total_area(v, f):
    a, b, c = v[f[:, 0]], v[f[:, 1]], v[f[:, 2]]
    return 0.5 * np.linalg.norm(np.cross(b - a, c - a), axis=1).sum()

def mean_curvature_error(v, f):
    # cotangent mean-curvature-normal: H_i = |Lv|_i / (4 A_i), the discrete
    # estimator nitrix.geometry.mean_curvature uses. Analytic truth on the
    # unit sphere is H = 1/r = 1, so we track |mean(H) - 1|.
    n = v.shape[0]
    Lv = np.zeros_like(v)
    area = np.zeros(n)
    for i, j, k in f:
        for a, b, c in ((i, j, k), (j, k, i), (k, i, j)):
            u, w = v[a] - v[c], v[b] - v[c]          # angle at vertex c
            cot = np.dot(u, w) / np.linalg.norm(np.cross(u, w))
            Lv[a] += cot * (v[a] - v[b])
            Lv[b] += cot * (v[b] - v[a])
        area[[i, j, k]] += np.linalg.norm(
            np.cross(v[j] - v[i], v[k] - v[i])) / 6.0  # barycentric third
    H = np.linalg.norm(Lv, axis=1) / (4.0 * area)
    return abs(H.mean() - 1.0)

v, f = icosahedron()
h, area_err, curv_err, meshes = [], [], [], []
for _ in range(6):
    h.append(np.linalg.norm(v[f[:, 0]] - v[f[:, 1]], axis=1).mean())
    area_err.append(abs(total_area(v, f) - 4 * np.pi) / (4 * np.pi))
    curv_err.append(mean_curvature_error(v, f))
    meshes.append((v.copy(), f.copy()))
    v, f = subdivide(v, f)
h, area_err, curv_err = map(np.array, (h, area_err, curv_err))

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(11, 4.4))
ax0 = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 1, projection='3d')
vv, ff = meshes[2]
ax0.add_collection3d(Poly3DCollection(
    vv[ff], facecolor='#9ecae1', edgecolor='#3182bd', linewidths=0.3, alpha=0.9))
ax0.set_xlim(-1, 1); ax0.set_ylim(-1, 1); ax0.set_zlim(-1, 1)
ax0.set_box_aspect((1, 1, 1)); ax0.set_axis_off()
ax0.set_title("icosphere (level 2): a mesh standing in\nfor the smooth unit sphere", fontsize=10)

ax1 = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 2)
ax1.loglog(h, area_err, 'o-', color='#3182bd', label=r'surface area $\to 4\pi r^2$')
# the level-0 icosahedron is vertex-transitive, so its mean curvature is exact
# by symmetry (machine zero); plot the genuine march from level 1 on.
ax1.loglog(h[1:], curv_err[1:], 's-', color='#e6550d', label=r'mean curvature $\to H = 1/r$')
ax1.loglog(h, area_err[0] * (h / h[0]) ** 2, 'k--', lw=1, alpha=0.6, label='slope 2')
ax1.set_xlabel('mean edge length $h$  (coarse -> fine)')
ax1.set_ylabel('error vs the analytic truth')
ax1.set_title('discrete quantities converge under refinement', fontsize=10)
ax1.legend(fontsize=8, loc='lower right'); ax1.grid(True, which='both', alpha=0.3)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The curve falling to the lower-left *is* the governing idea made visible. Every
later page returns to it: when we claim curvature, the heat kernel, or a distance
transform "is correct," we mean a curve like this one.

## Validation philosophy: oracles, not bit-parity {#sec-validation}

If two correct programs computed the same geometric quantity, would their outputs
be equal to the last bit? No — and *expecting* them to be is the most common way to
mis-judge a geometry library. This section says why, because it frames every
"validation" claim in the unit.

The reason is exactly the discrete-approximates-smooth idea. There is no single
"correct" discrete Laplacian or discrete curvature; there are *many* valid
discretisations, each making different but legitimate choices (cotangent weights
vs. a fitted quadric; marching tetrahedra vs. marching cubes; a forward vs. a
central difference). They are different functions of the mesh — they will *not*
agree bit-for-bit — yet they all converge to the *same continuum*. Bit-parity
demands agreement on the part that is an arbitrary modelling choice; convergence
demands agreement only on the part that is real. The right target is the latter.

So `nitrix` validates geometry against **two oracles**, and never against another
implementation's bits.

**1. The analytic oracle.** On a shape with closed-form geometry — the unit sphere,
an analytic signed-distance field — the continuous answer is known exactly, so the
test is "does the discrete answer approach it?" Concretely: a sphere mesh's mean
curvature should approach $H = 1/r$ and its Gaussian curvature $K = 1/r^2$; the
summed angle defect should approach Gauss–Bonnet's $\sum_i K A_i = 4\pi$ for a
genus-0 surface; a mesh extracted from the SDF of a sphere of radius $r$ should
have area approaching $4\pi r^2$; a rigid motion should produce *zero* distortion.
These are crisp because the icosphere's uniform valence makes the discrete sums
clean. The shipped curvature suite, for instance, hits $H = 1.0000$ and
Gauss–Bonnet $= 4\pi$ on the sphere oracle exactly.

**2. Real-data class and correlation** — the "metrics↔ITK" posture. On a real
cortical surface there is no closed form, so we compare against an established tool
(FreeSurfer overlays) by **sign and correlation**, not equality. Mean curvature
should be *negative in sulci and positive on gyri* (a sign/class check) and should
*correlate strongly* with FreeSurfer's `?h.curv` — and when it does not reach
correlation $1.0$, the gap is *measured and accounted for*, not waved away. In the
shipped suite the anti-correlation with `?h.curv` on fsaverage5 is $-0.90$, and the
gap from unity is fully explained: FreeSurfer smooths `curv` by default (matched
light smoothing lifts the agreement to $-0.95$, then it decays under
over-smoothing — the tell-tale signature of "the reference is a lightly-smoothed
version of what we compute"); the two use different discrete estimators (cotangent
mean-curvature normal vs. a fitted quadric); and the overlay is itself
group-averaged and resampled. Each of these is a legitimate reason for two correct
programs to differ — exactly the reason bit-parity is the wrong gate.

@fig-foundations-validation shows both halves: the analytic-oracle convergence of
two valid estimators, and the correlation (not identity) gate against a reference.

```{python}
#| label: fig-foundations-validation
#| fig-cap: "The two-oracle posture. Left: two different-but-valid discretisations of the same derivative (forward, first order; central, second order) both converge to the analytic oracle $\\cos x$, at different rates — they never agree with each other bit-for-bit, yet both are correct. Right: against a reference tool the right acceptance gate is high correlation and matched sign across many test cases, not the unattainable $y=x$ bit-parity line."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# --- analytic oracle: d/dx sin(x) = cos(x), two valid discretisations -------
x0, truth = 1.0, np.cos(1.0)
hs = np.logspace(-0.3, -3.0, 14)
err_central = np.array([abs((np.sin(x0 + h) - np.sin(x0 - h)) / (2 * h) - truth) for h in hs])
err_forward = np.array([abs((np.sin(x0 + h) - np.sin(x0)) / h - truth) for h in hs])

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4.4))
axL.loglog(hs, err_forward, 'o-', color='#e6550d', label='forward diff (1st order)')
axL.loglog(hs, err_central, 's-', color='#3182bd', label='central diff (2nd order)')
axL.loglog(hs, err_forward[0] * (hs / hs[0]),    'k:',  lw=1, alpha=.6, label='slope 1')
axL.loglog(hs, err_central[0] * (hs / hs[0])**2, 'k--', lw=1, alpha=.6, label='slope 2')
axL.set_xlabel('grid spacing $h$  (coarse -> fine)')
axL.set_ylabel(r'$|$discrete $-$ analytic oracle$|$')
axL.set_title('two valid discretisations,\none continuum truth', fontsize=10)
axL.legend(fontsize=8); axL.grid(True, which='both', alpha=.3)

# --- real-data posture: correlation + sign, not bit-parity ------------------
n = 200
latent = rng.normal(size=n)                       # the continuum quantity
ours = latent + 0.06 * rng.normal(size=n)         # our estimator
ref  = latent + 0.06 * rng.normal(size=n)         # a reference tool
r = np.corrcoef(ours, ref)[0, 1]
axR.scatter(ours, ref, s=14, alpha=.6, color='#31a354', edgecolor='none')
lim = [min(ours.min(), ref.min()), max(ours.max(), ref.max())]
axR.plot(lim, lim, 'k--', lw=1, alpha=.5, label='$y=x$ (bit-parity, unattainable)')
axR.set_xlabel('nitrix estimate (per test case)')
axR.set_ylabel('reference tool estimate')
axR.set_title(f'class / correlation gate (r = {r:.2f}),\nnot bit-parity', fontsize=10)
axR.legend(fontsize=8, loc='upper left'); axR.grid(True, alpha=.3)
axR.set_aspect('equal', 'box')
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

A practical corollary closes this section. When you write or read a test in this
unit, ask "which oracle?" — an exact analytic identity (assert closeness to the
closed form, tightening with refinement) or a real-data class/correlation (assert
the sign pattern and a correlation floor, with the residual gap explained). If a
test demands bit-parity with another tool, it is testing the wrong thing.

## Execution classes: the taxonomy that governs the code {#sec-classes}

Every public symbol in the geometry suite is tagged, in its docstring, with one of
**three execution classes**. The classification is not bureaucratic — it tells you
the single most important operational fact about a routine: *can you call it inside
a jitted, differentiated optimisation loop, or not?* Get this wrong and your
optimiser either fails to compile or silently stops being differentiable.

The classes:

- **DIFF-JAX** — a jittable *and* differentiable kernel. Pure array math with
  shapes fixed at trace time; safe to `jit`, `vmap`, `grad`, and to place inside a
  `lax.fori_loop`. These are the workhorses of any surface optimiser: `face_areas`,
  `vertex_areas`, `mesh_mass_matrix`, `compute_vertex_normals`, `mean_curvature`,
  `gaussian_curvature`, `principal_curvatures`, `areal_distortion`,
  `strain_distortion`, `surface_smooth`, `deform_to_sdf`, `inflate_surface`,
  `ribbon_map`, `spatial_gradient`, and the apply-seam `apply_operator`.
- **HOST-CTOR** — host-side *construction* that emits a `Mesh` or `ELL` whose shape
  is **data-dependent**. The output size cannot be known until the data is
  inspected (how many vertices does a level set have? how many neighbours does a
  vertex have?), so the work runs in NumPy on the host, *outside* any trace, and
  hands JAX a concrete array. Examples: `icosphere` and the icosphere-hierarchy
  constructors, `mesh_k_ring_adjacency`, `mesh_cotangent_laplacian`,
  `mesh_mass_matrix` *assembly*, the `SectionedELL` emission, `marching_cubes`,
  `mesh_to_sdf`, `euler_characteristic`, `genus`.
- **HOST-QA** — host-side *analysis or query* that is not on a differentiable path:
  combinatorial questions answered with spatial-acceleration structures.
  `find_self_intersections` (a uniform-hash broad-phase plus a Möller narrow-phase)
  and `remove_self_intersections` are the canonical pair.

The boundary that decides DIFF-JAX vs. HOST is **shape**. JAX traces a function to
a fixed computation graph, so every array shape must be known at trace time. A
curvature value has the same shape as the vertex set — known — so curvature is
DIFF-JAX. A level set has as many vertices as the volume happens to produce —
*unknown until you look* — so marching cubes must be HOST-CTOR.

::: {.callout-important title="The load-bearing rule"}
**A HOST op (CTOR or QA) must never be called inside a jitted `fori_loop`.** Inside
a trace there is no host, no Python list-building, no data-dependent shape; calling
one there either fails to compile or, worse, silently bakes in a stale value. The
discipline: optimisers regularise *inside* the loop with **DIFF-JAX ops only**
(Laplacian smoothing, area penalties, normal steps), and any **HOST** construction
or cleanup (building the operator, extracting a mesh, removing
self-intersections) runs **before or after** the loop — never within it. When an
algorithm genuinely needs periodic host cleanup, it confines that cleanup to
*between* jitted chunks, not inside them.
:::

@fig-foundations-classes is the decision tree in one picture: ask of any op whether
its output shape is data-dependent, then whether it is pure array math, and read
off the class and the "callable inside jit?" verdict.

```{python}
#| label: fig-foundations-classes
#| fig-cap: "The execution-class decision tree. The first question — does the output shape depend on the data? — separates the differentiable DIFF-JAX kernels (jittable, the body of any surface optimiser) from the host-side HOST-CTOR builders (data-dependent shape) and HOST-QA queries. The banner is the one rule that keeps a differentiable pipeline correct: HOST ops live outside the jitted loop."
#| code-fold: true
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import FancyBboxPatch, FancyArrowPatch

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5.6))
ax.set_xlim(0, 12); ax.set_ylim(0, 10); ax.axis('off')

def box(x, y, w, h, text, fc, ec='#333333', fs=9, weight='normal'):
    ax.add_patch(FancyBboxPatch((x, y), w, h,
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.08,rounding_size=0.15", fc=fc, ec=ec, lw=1.3))
    ax.text(x + w / 2, y + h / 2, text, ha='center', va='center', fontsize=fs, weight=weight)

def arrow(x0, y0, x1, y1, label=None, lx=0, ly=0):
    ax.add_patch(FancyArrowPatch((x0, y0), (x1, y1), arrowstyle='-|>',
        mutation_scale=14, lw=1.2, color='#555555'))
    if label:
        ax.text((x0 + x1) / 2 + lx, (y0 + y1) / 2 + ly, label,
                fontsize=8.5, ha='center', va='center', style='italic')

box(4.0, 8.6, 4.0, 1.0, "Does the op's OUTPUT SHAPE\ndepend on the data?", '#fff2cc', fs=9.5, weight='bold')
arrow(4.0, 9.1, 1.8, 7.4, 'no', lx=-0.5, ly=0.3)
box(0.2, 6.4, 3.2, 1.0, "Pure array math,\nstatic shapes?", '#fff2cc', fs=9)
arrow(8.0, 9.1, 10.2, 7.4, 'yes', lx=0.5, ly=0.3)
box(8.4, 6.0, 3.4, 1.4, "HOST-CTOR\nhost build emits Mesh/ELL\n(marching_cubes, genus,\nSectionedELL emission)", '#fde0dd', fs=8.5, weight='bold')
arrow(1.8, 6.4, 1.0, 4.6, 'yes', lx=-0.45, ly=0.2)
box(0.0, 3.4, 3.4, 1.2, "DIFF-JAX\njittable + differentiable\n(curvature, areas, mass,\nsmoothing, deform_to_sdf)", '#d5efd5', fs=8.5, weight='bold')
arrow(2.6, 6.4, 5.1, 4.6, 'no -- a host query', lx=0.9, ly=0.2)
box(4.0, 3.4, 3.6, 1.2, "HOST-QA\nhost analysis, not in a\ndiff path (self-intersection,\nBVH / hash queries)", '#dbeaf6', fs=8.5, weight='bold')
box(0.2, 0.7, 11.6, 1.4,
    "THE LOAD-BEARING RULE:  a HOST op (CTOR or QA) must NEVER be called inside a jitted fori_loop.\n"
    "Optimisers regularise with DIFF-JAX ops only;  HOST construction / cleanup runs BEFORE or AFTER the loop.",
    '#f0f0f0', ec='#990000', fs=9.5, weight='bold')
ax.text(1.7, 3.25, "callable inside jit  YES", fontsize=8, ha='center', color='#1a7a1a')
ax.text(5.8, 3.25, "callable inside jit  NO", fontsize=8, ha='center', color='#a33')
ax.text(10.1, 5.85, "callable inside jit  NO", fontsize=8, ha='center', color='#a33')
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

One subtlety worth naming now. An operator like the cotangent Laplacian is
**built** on the host (HOST-CTOR — the neighbour structure and the per-edge weights
need data-dependent assembly) but **applied** in pure JAX: the resulting `ELL`
flows through `apply_operator` as a DIFF-JAX matvec. So the *construction* sits
outside the loop and the *application* sits inside it — the two halves naturally
land on the right side of the rule. (One consequence: the host-built ELL's weights
are NumPy constants, so they do not carry a gradient with respect to vertex
positions. Where an algorithm needs to differentiate *through* the cotangent
weights — `mean_curvature` does — it uses a JAX-native per-face cotangent apply
instead of the host-built ELL, the two agreeing to about $10^{-4}$.)

## Device discipline: cuSolver-free, XLA, differentiable {#sec-devices}

The geometry tier makes three device-level bets that shape its whole design. They
are worth understanding before you read any routine, because they explain *why* the
code looks the way it does.

**1. cuSolver-free by construction.** On real hardware the dense GPU LAPACK stack
(cuSolver) is not always trustworthy: certain CUDA / JAX combinations ship a broken
solver handle in which `eigh` / `qr` / `cholesky` / `solve` / `inv` / `svd` all
fail at runtime while the rest of the device works fine. Rather than depend on it,
the suite avoids it: small linear algebra uses **closed-form** small inverses and
determinants and **2×2 eigenvalues** (principal curvatures are
$\kappa = H \pm \sqrt{H^2 - K}$, a closed form, never a call to an eigensolver);
large systems use **matrix-free conjugate gradient** (`linalg.krylov.cg`), which
needs only matrix–vector products, never a factorisation — the backward-Euler
smoothing solve $(M + tL)x = M x_0$ rides it. Where a dense factorisation is truly
unavoidable, it goes through the **`safe_*` seam** (`safe_eigh`, `safe_inv`,
`safe_cholesky`, `safe_cho_solve`, `safe_solve` in `linalg._solver`): each probes a
tiny GPU call once, and *latches to CPU* the first time the GPU call actually fails,
moving the result back to the originating device. The fallback is announced, never
silent — a wedged solver pool degrades to a correct CPU computation instead of a
crash.

**2. XLA, not Pallas — there are no custom kernels for this tier.** The mesh and
semiring layer lowers onto `semiring_ell_matmul`, whose core operation is a
**gather**: for each output row, read the feature rows of that row's neighbours
(`lax.gather`) and reduce them under a semiring. XLA lowers this gather/scatter +
`fori_loop` pattern efficiently on GPU. A custom Triton (Pallas) kernel cannot — in
the pinned JAX version the Pallas GPU backend does not lower the `gather` primitive
at all (it raises `Unimplemented primitive in Pallas GPU lowering: gather`). So the
substrate bet is **XLA**, and the suite deliberately ships **no** custom kernels for
the mesh/semiring tier. (The full rationale, and the conditions under which that
might change, live in [the backend page](performance.qmd#sec-backend).)

**3. Everything stays differentiable.** Two mechanisms make the discrete pipeline
end-to-end differentiable. First, the data containers are **registered pytrees**:
`Mesh`, `ELL`, and `SectionedELL` flatten into their array children, so a `Mesh`
flows through `jit` / `vmap` / `grad` as a first-class operand — `jax.grad` of a
loss over a `Mesh` returns a `Mesh`-structured cotangent, with the gradient
populating `vertices` (the `faces` integer topology takes a zero cotangent). One
type is *consciously not* registered: `IcosphereHierarchy`, whose parent tables are
host-side construction metadata that never legitimately crosses a trace boundary.
Second, the few routines built around an inner solve or fixed point expose a
correct gradient through **implicit differentiation** — a `custom_vjp` that
differentiates the *solution* of the system rather than unrolling the iteration
(the eigensolver and the fixed-point solver both do this). Together these mean you
can put a discrete-geometry computation inside a loss and optimise the underlying
surface by gradient descent.

These three bets interlock with the execution classes of the previous section: the
DIFF-JAX kernels are exactly the ones written to be cuSolver-free and XLA-lowerable,
and the pytree registration is what lets a `Mesh` be a DIFF-JAX operand at all.

## Conventions: the small print that prevents large bugs {#sec-conventions}

A handful of representation choices run through the entire unit. They are easy to
state and expensive to get wrong, so we fix them once here and use them everywhere.

**Index space.** Grid coordinates are **index space**: the position of grid cell
$i$ *is* $i$ (physical position is `index * spacing`). Routines that care about
physical units take an explicit `spacing` argument — `spatial_gradient`,
`marching_cubes`, `mesh_to_sdf`, `ribbon_map` all do — and apply it as a scale on
top of the index grid. There is no hidden affine; what you index is where you are.

**Channels-last.** Multi-channel arrays put the feature axis **last**. A per-vertex
field is `(..., n, d)` with the $d$ features trailing and any leading axes treated
as batch; a scalar field uses a trailing singleton `(..., n, 1)`. The apply-seam
`apply_operator(op, x)` follows this exactly, vmapping over the leading batch axes
and contracting the `(n, d)` core. (`spatial_gradient` is the one place you move a
channel axis to the *front* before calling, because there every trailing axis is
spatial.)

**`indexing='ij'` for multi-axis grids.** Every `meshgrid` over a multi-axis grid
uses matrix indexing, so axis 0 is the first spatial dimension, matching the array
layout — the volume code (`marching_cubes`, `mesh_to_sdf`) and the interpolators
all build their coordinate grids this way. The alternative (`'xy'`) silently
transposes the first two axes and is a classic source of flipped volumes.

**fp64 host assembly, then cast back.** Numerically sensitive *construction* — most
of all the cotangent weights, where a near-degenerate triangle can blow up a
$\cot$ — is computed in **float64 on the host**, then cast back to the working
dtype (typically fp32) for the JAX matvec. `mesh_cotangent_laplacian` does exactly
this: it assembles `values` in float64 and returns them in `mesh.vertices.dtype`.
The principle is to spend the precision where the conditioning is worst (assembly)
and run the bulk arithmetic at the cheaper working precision.

**The `Mesh` pytree.** A mesh is `Mesh(vertices, faces)` — both are array
*children* of the pytree (not static aux). `vertices` is float and carries the
gradient; `faces` is integer topology and carries through with a zero cotangent.
Making `faces` a child (rather than static metadata) is deliberate: a large integer
array as static aux would be unhashable and would force a recompile for every
distinct topology. The usual optimiser pattern grads with respect to `vertices`
directly and rebuilds the `Mesh` inside the loss; differentiating the *whole* mesh
needs `jax.grad(f, allow_int=True)` to admit the integer `faces` leaf.

With index space, channels-last, `'ij'`, the fp64-assembly rule, and the `Mesh`
pytree in hand, the rest of the unit reads without surprises. The next page picks up
the operator layer in earnest: [ELL storage and the semiring
matvec](operators.qmd#sec-ell) that every mesh computation rides on.

## Further reading

- Crane, K. (2018). *Discrete Differential Geometry: An Applied Introduction.*
  Course notes, Carnegie Mellon University. The single best entry point for the
  discrete-approximates-smooth viewpoint and the cotangent/angle-defect operators.
- Botsch, M., Kobbelt, L., Pauly, M., Alliez, P., & Lévy, B. (2010). *Polygon Mesh
  Processing.* A K Peters. The practitioner's reference for mesh representations,
  smoothing, and remeshing.
- Meyer, M., Desbrun, M., Schröder, P., & Barr, A. H. (2003). *Discrete
  Differential-Geometry Operators for Triangulated 2-Manifolds.* In Visualization
  and Mathematics III, Springer. The source of the mixed-Voronoi area and the
  cotangent mean-curvature normal used in `vertex_areas` and `mean_curvature`.
- do Carmo, M. P. (1976). *Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces.*
  Prentice-Hall. The smooth theory the discrete operators approximate — mean and
  Gaussian curvature, the Gauss–Bonnet theorem.
- LeVeque, R. J. (2007). *Finite Difference Methods for Ordinary and Partial
  Differential Equations.* SIAM. Background on the order-of-accuracy / convergence
  ideas behind the finite-difference figure.

## See also

- The geometry suite's own design contracts (execution classes in §2.5; the device
  posture in §2.6):
  [`docs/design/geometry-suite.md`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/docs/design/geometry-suite.md).
- Source — the `Mesh` pytree, the icosphere, and the mesh operator/measure layer:
  [`src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/mesh.py).
- Source — the geometry package surface (the full public API this page surveys):
  [`src/nitrix/geometry/__init__.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/geometry/__init__.py).
- Source — the cuSolver-robust `safe_*` device seam:
  [`src/nitrix/linalg/_solver.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/linalg/_solver.py).
- Source — the format-agnostic apply-seam:
  [`src/nitrix/sparse/_apply.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/sparse/_apply.py).
- Next in reading order: [the operator layer](operators.qmd#sec-ell), then
  [the cotangent Laplacian](laplace-beltrami.qmd#sec-cotangent) and
  [curvature](curvature.qmd#sec-mean). Sibling unit:
  [the `nitrix.stats` foundations page](../foundations.qmd).
